speaker: doctor
text: Good morning, Mr. Thompson. Let's start with your vitals — your temperature today is 36.4 °C, which is within the normal range.

speaker: patient
text: Good morning, Doctor. Thanks for seeing me. I’ve been feeling a bit more fatigued than usual lately, and some shortness of breath when climbing stairs.

speaker: doctor
text: I see — your systolic blood pressure is 98 mmHg today, which is on the lower side but not critically so. How have you been feeling with your blood pressure medications?

speaker: patient
text: Mostly okay, though I did feel lightheaded once last week after standing up quickly. Otherwise, no major issues.

speaker: doctor
text: Your fasting blood glucose came back at 12.8 mmol/L — that’s significantly elevated and suggests poor glycemic control. Are you monitoring your sugars regularly?

speaker: patient
text: I check occasionally — mostly in the mornings. Last time I checked before coming in, it was around 9.2, but I wasn’t fasting then.

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on Lisinopril 10mg once daily for blood pressure and cardiac protection. Given your diagnosis of Coronary Artery Disease, this remains appropriate for now.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I’ve been taking it consistently every morning with breakfast. No side effects like cough or swelling.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin level today is 152 g/L — solid, well within the normal range for men. That supports good oxygen-carrying capacity.

speaker: patient
text: That’s reassuring. Though I did notice my gums bled a little more easily over the past two weeks — nothing severe, but unusual for me.

speaker: doctor
text: Any bruising, nosebleeds, or changes in stool color? We’ll want to rule out any hematologic or GI contributors.

speaker: patient
text: No black stools or nosebleeds, but I did have one episode of dark, tarry stool about ten days ago — I didn’t mention it earlier because it only happened once.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s important — we’ll need to investigate further. Let’s schedule a fecal occult blood test and consider an urgent gastroenterology referral.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, yes — I’m a bit worried. Also, my energy has dropped noticeably since last month, even with the same activity level.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your symptoms and the lab trends, we’ll repeat a full CBC and iron studies next week. Your fatigue and bleeding could point to chronic blood loss.

speaker: patient
text: Makes sense. I’ve also been drinking more water and urinating more frequently — could that tie into the high glucose?

speaker: doctor
text: Absolutely — polyuria and polydipsia are classic signs of hyperglycemia. We’ll adjust your diabetes management plan today, likely adding metformin.

speaker: doctor
text: Interestingly, your repeat hemoglobin just came back at 124 g/L — down from 152 g/L earlier today. That drop confirms concern for acute or subacute blood loss.

speaker: patient
text: Oh — so that explains the fatigue and paleness I’ve noticed. I thought maybe it was just stress.

speaker: patient
text: By the way, I double-checked my temperature this morning before leaving home — it was exactly the same as the reading you took earlier: 36.4 °C.
